1: ṣaw lĕbêtekā — "put your house in order/command your household" (ṣāwâ, to command; literally "command to your house"). This is the standard phrase for dying preparations — making arrangements for succession, inheritance, and burial. kî mēt ʾattâ wĕlōʾ tiḥyeh — "for you shall die and not live" — the death sentence is absolute and conditional-free. YHWH speaks it through the prophet without softening.
2: wayyāsseb Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾet-pānāyw ʾel-haqqîr — "Hezekiah turned his face to the wall" — the physical posture of private, intense prayer. The turn away from the sickroom audience toward the wall is both practical (privacy) and symbolic (orientation toward YHWH, away from human society). wayyitpallel ʾel-Yhwh — "and prayed to YHWH" — the prayer follows immediately.
3: hithallaktî lĕpāneykā beʾĕmet — "I have walked before you in truth/faithfulness" (ʾemet, H571, truth, reliability, fidelity — one of the covenant character terms). ûbĕlēḇ šālēm — "with a wholehearted/complete heart" (šālēm, complete, undivided). wĕhaṭṭôb bĕʿêneykā ʿāśîtî — "I have done what is good in your eyes." Hezekiah's prayer appeals to covenant fidelity, not merely divine mercy — he presents his life as evidence of faithfulness. wayyibkĕ Ḥizqiyyāhû bĕkî gādôl — "Hezekiah wept with great weeping."
4: The divine response comes wayhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾel-Yĕšaʿyāhû — "the word of YHWH came to Isaiah" — before the prophet has even left the middle court (ḥāṣēr hattîkônâ). The speed of divine response is emphasized: the prayer was heard immediately. Isaiah had delivered a death sentence (Isaiah 38:1) and now receives a reversal from YHWH — both words are YHWH's word, showing that divine pronouncements can be conditional on human response.
5: šûb wĕʾāmartā ʾel-Ḥizqiyyāhû — "return and say to Hezekiah" — Isaiah is sent back. šāmaʿtî ʾet-tĕpillatekā rāʾîtî ʾet-dimʿāteykā — "I have heard your prayer, I have seen your tears" — the two verbs address the two modes of Hezekiah's petition: spoken prayer and wept tears. Both were received. hinnî yōsip ʿal-yāmeykā ḥāmēš ʿeśrēh šānâ — "I will add fifteen years to your days." The specific number makes the promise verifiable.
6: ûmikkaP melek ʾaššûr ʾaṣṣîlĕkā wĕʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "and I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria" — the healing is coupled with the deliverance of Jerusalem, connecting Hezekiah's personal crisis to the national crisis of Isaiah 37. The two promises are simultaneous: king healed + city defended. wĕgannōtî ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "I will defend this city" — repeating Isaiah 37:35.
7: The sign: wĕzeh-lĕkā hāʾôt mēʾēt Yhwh — "and this is the sign for you from YHWH." The sign authenticates the promise before it is fulfilled — it is evidence designed to produce faith, not just information. The reversal of the sun's shadow on the sundial of Ahaz (maʿălôt ʾāḥāz) — moving backward rather than forward — is an extraordinary astronomical sign. The shadow going backward (šāḇâ hāṣēl ʿeśer maʿălôt) is a temporal reversal that corresponds to the temporal gift of fifteen added years.
8: hinnî mēšîḇ ʾet-ṣel hammaʿălôt — "behold, I will make the shadow retreat on the steps" — the causative of šûḇ (return). The sign is explicitly YHWH's action (ʾănî, first person), not a natural phenomenon misinterpreted. The sun returning ten steps on the sundial of Ahaz (maʿălôt ʾāḥāz) is a specific, localized event that authenticates a specific, localized promise. The sign does not announce cosmic reversal but personal restoration.
9: miktāb lĕḥizqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ bĕḥalōtô wayyeḥî mēḥolyô — "the writing of Hezekiah king of Judah when he was ill and recovered from his illness" — the superscription of the Song of Hezekiah (Isaiah 38:10-20). miktāb (H4385) can mean a written text, inscription, or poem — a form used for composed literary pieces (cf. Psalms 16:1; Isaiah 56:1 miktam). The psalm is a thanksgiving composed in retrospect from the perspective of survival.
10: ʾănî ʾāmartî bidmî yāmay — "I said in the noontide/prime of my days" — dĕmî (H1824) suggests the still point, the midpoint — cut off in the middle of his years. ʾēlĕkâ bĕšaʿărê šĕʾôl — "I will go to the gates of Sheol" — the image of Sheol as a city with gates (cf. Psalms 107:18; Job 38:17; Matthew 16:18). puqqadtî yeter šĕnōtāy — "I am deprived of the rest of my years" (pāqad, here in the pual — to be visited in deprivation, counted off, assigned to loss).
11: ʾāmartî lōʾ-ʾerʾeh Yāh Yāh bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm — "I said I will not see Yah, Yah, in the land of the living" — the doubled Yāh (the short form of YHWH) intensifies the loss: not just life but the vision of YHWH in the assembly is what Hezekiah mourns. lōʾ-ʾabbîṭ ʾādām ʿôd ʿim-yôšĕbê ḥāḏel — "I shall look no more upon man among the inhabitants of the world" (ḥāḏel, H2309, the cessation/world of the deceased — an unusual term for the underworld).
12: dôrî nissaʿ wĕniglâ minnî kĕʾōhel rōʿî — "my dwelling/generation is pulled up and removed from me like a shepherd's tent" (dôr, H1755, here in its rare meaning of "circle/dwelling/generation"). The tent imagery: a nomad's temporary shelter that can be struck and moved — death is the collapse and removal of one's dwelling. qippadtî kāʾōrēg ḥayyay — "like a weaver I roll up my life" (qāpad, to roll up or draw together — the life as a finished web being rolled up from the loom).
13: šiwitî ʿad-bōqer — "I waited until morning" — the long night of illness. kaʾărî kēn yĕšabbēr kol-ʿaṣmōtāy — "like a lion he breaks all my bones" — the illness personified as a lion crushing bones. The identification of YHWH as the source of the illness (the implied subject of "he breaks") reflects the ancient Hebrew theology that sickness was within YHWH's sovereign permission, not outside it — which is precisely why prayer to YHWH can heal.
14: kĕsûs ʿāgûr kēn ʾăṣappṣēp — "like a swallow or crane (sûs ʿāgûr) I chirp" — the plaintive bird-cry as a metaphor for weak, inarticulate prayer. The species identification of sûs and ʿāgûr is debated: possibly "swallow and crane" or "swift and turtledove." ʾehgeh kĕyônâ — "I moan like a dove." Three bird-sounds (chirping, twittering, moaning) describe the wordless quality of extreme illness prayer. dallû ʿênay lammārôm — "my eyes are weary looking upward" — exhausted, persistent upward orientation.
15: mā-ʾaddabbēr wĕʾāmar lî — "what shall I say, since he has both spoken and acted?" — Hezekiah is silenced by the reality of what YHWH has done. ʾeddaddeh kol-šĕnôtay ʿal-mar napšî — "I must walk carefully all my years because of the bitterness of my soul" — the surviving years are now lived under the shadow of the near-death, with a chastened and careful step (dādâ, to walk slowly/carefully).
16: ʾădōnāy ʿălêhem yiḥyû — "O Lord, by these things men live, and in all these is the life of my spirit." The "things" refer to the divine words, promises, and judgments that filled the psalm. Human life is sustained not merely by physical health but by the content of YHWH's address to his people. wĕtaḥălîmēnî wĕhaḥăyēnî — "restore me to health and give me life" — the prayer for healing is embedded within the broader theological vision of life as YHWH's gift.
17: hinnēh lĕšālôm mar-lî mar — "behold, it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness" — the retrospective theology of suffering. The bitterness proved to be the instrument of peace (šālôm). wĕʾattâ ḥāšaqtā napšî miššaḥat bĕlî — "but you have loved/desired (ḥāšaq, H2836, to delight in, to be attached to) my soul from the pit of destruction." kî hišlaḵtā ʾaḥărê gēwĕkā kol-ḥăṭāʾāy — "you have cast all my sins behind your back" — the forgiveness of sins coupled with the healing of the body.
18: kî lōʾ šĕʾôl tôdekkā māwet yĕhallĕlekkā — "for Sheol does not thank you, death does not praise you" — the theological argument for healing from the perspective of worship. Those who go down to the pit (yôrĕdê bôr) do not hope for YHWH's faithfulness (ʾel-ʾămittekā). Hezekiah's argument is not that he deserves to live but that the dead cannot praise YHWH — and YHWH desires the praise of his creatures. This praise-theology argument for healing is unique in the Hebrew Bible.
19: ḥāy ḥāy hûʾ yôdekā kāmônî hayyôm — "the living, the living, they praise you, as I do today" — the doubled ḥāy (living, living) mirrors the doubled Yāh Yāh of Isaiah 38:11 — two anguished calls to YHWH now become two declarations of living praise. ʾāḇ lĕḇānîm yôdîaʿ ʾel-ʾămittekā — "a father makes known to his children your faithfulness" — intergenerational transmission of praise is only possible through continued life. Hezekiah's argument climaxes here.
20: Yhwh lĕhôšîʿēnî — "YHWH will save me" — the concluding confidence statement. ûnĕgînôtay nĕnaggen kol-yĕmê ḥayyênû ʿal-bêt Yhwh — "we will play my songs on stringed instruments all the days of our life at the house of YHWH." The plural nĕnaggen (we will play) and ḥayyênû (our life) suggest communal temple worship — Hezekiah's individual healing becomes the occasion for shared liturgical music. The temple is the site of realized salvation.
21: wayyōʾmer Yĕšaʿyāhû — "Isaiah said" — a surprising appendix after the psalm. yiśśĕʾû dĕbelet tĕʾēnîm — "let them take a cake of figs" (debelet, H1690, a fig-cake, a pressed lump of dried figs). The medical instruction — applying a poultice of figs to the boil (šĕḥîn) — reflects ancient Near Eastern medical practice where figs were used therapeutically. The miraculous healing and the natural medical treatment coexist without tension in the narrative.
22: wayyōʾmer Ḥizqiyyāhû mah ʾôt kî ʾeʿĕleh bêt Yhwh — "Hezekiah had said, 'What is the sign that I shall go up to the house of YHWH?'" — this retrospective verse appears to have been displaced from its logical position (after Isaiah 38:7, where the sign is given). Its placement here may be an editorial note showing that Hezekiah's recovery was validated by his ability to return to the temple — the ultimate mark of restored communion with YHWH.
